supabase_cases.case_summary:
According to the Complaint, Coinbase Global, Inc. offers (i) consumers their primary financial account for the cryptoeconomy, (ii) institutions a full-service prime brokerage platform with access to deep pools of liquidity across the crypto marketplace, and (iii) developers a suite of products granting access to the Company’s ecosystem. This class action was filed against Coinbase and two of its Officers.
The Complaint alleges that, throughout the Class Period, Defendants made materially false and/or misleading statements and/or failed to disclose that: (1) In 2020, the United Kingdom's Financial Conduct Authority ("FCA") had deemed efforts by CB Payments Limited ("CBPL"), part of the Coinbase Group, to prevent criminals from using its platform to be inadequate; (2) as a result, the FCA reached an agreement with CBPL, which put requirements in place that were designed to prevent high risk customers from using CBPL's platform; (3) CBPL then breached that agreement, which resulted in 13,416 high risk individuals receiving services; (4) the foregoing resulted in an undisclosed heightened regulatory risk; and (5) as a result, Defendants' statements about its business, operations and prospects were materially false and misleading and/or lacked a reasonable basis at all relevant times.
On January 27, 2025, the Court issued an Order appointing Co-Lead Plaintiffs and Counsel. Co-Lead Plaintiffs filed a Consolidated Complaint on April 4.
On June 2, 2025, the Court issued an Order consolidating cases. All future docketing was ordered to be done in the Lead Case 24-CV-04850.

According to the Complaint, Coinbase Global, Inc. provides financial infrastructure and technology products and services for the cryptocurrency economy (or “cryptoeconomy”) in the U.S. and internationally. The Company purportedly offers the primary financial account in the cryptoeconomy for retailers, a marketplace with a pool of liquidity for transacting in crypto assets for institutions, and technology and services that enable ecosystem partners to build crypto-based applications and securely accept crypto assets as payment.
The Complaint alleges that throughout the Class Period, Defendants made materially false and misleading statements regarding the Company’s business, operations, and compliance policies.  Specifically, the Complaint alleges Defendants made false and/or misleading statements and/or failed to disclose that: (i) Coinbase custodially held crypto assets on behalf of its customers, which assets Coinbase knew or recklessly disregarded could qualify as the property of a bankruptcy estate, making those assets potentially subject to bankruptcy proceedings in which Coinbase’s customers would be treated as the Company’s general unsecured creditors; (ii) Coinbase allowed Americans to trade digital assets that Coinbase knew or recklessly disregarded should have been registered as securities with the SEC; (iii) the foregoing conduct subjected the Company to a heightened risk of regulatory and governmental scrutiny and enforcement action; and (iv) as a result, the Company’s public statements were materially false and misleading at all relevant times.
On December 12, 2022, the Court issued an Order consolidating cases and appointing Lead Plaintiff and Counsel. All future docketing was ordered to be done in the lead case 22-CV-04915.
Lead Plaintiff filed a consolidated Complaint on February 22, 2023. On May 10, Lead Plaintiff filed an amended consolidated Complaint. On July 20, Lead Plaintiff filed a second amended consolidated Complaint. Defendants filed a Motion to Dismiss the second amended consolidated Complaint on December 21, 2023.
On September 5, 2024, the Court issued an Order granting in part and denying in part Defendants' Motion to Dismiss. Plaintiff was given leave to amend the Complaint.

According to the Complaint, Coinbase Global, Inc. is a cryptocurrency exchange. In November 2021, Coinbase first allowed trading of GYEN stablecoins.
The Complaint alleges that Defendants offered or sold GYEN by means of a prospectus or communication that was materially misleading and that members of the Class who acquired the GYEN stablecoins pursuant to the offering materials and communications sustained substantial damages in connection with their acquisition.
On August 9, 2022, the Court issued an Order appointing Lead Plaintiffs and Counsel. Lead Plaintiffs filed an amended Complaint on August 25. Defendants filed a Motion to Dismiss the amended Complaint on October 6. On August 2, 2023, the Court issued an Order granting Plaintiffs' Motion to file an amended Complaint and denied Defendants' Motion to Dismiss.
On September 25, 2023, the case transferred in to the Southern District of New York under Docket 23-CV-08431.
Lead Plaintiffs filed a second amended Complaint on April 8, 2024. Defendants filed a Motion to dismiss the second amended Complaint on May 23. On February 17, 2025, the Court issued an Order granting in part and denying in part Defendants' Motion to Dismiss.

According to the Complaint, Coinbase Global, Inc. is a cryptocurrency exchange.
The Complaint alleges that, despite the fact that the Coinbase Digital Asset Platforms, as operated by Coinbase, meet the definition of an “exchange” under federal securities laws, Coinbase has not registered the Coinbase Digital Asset Platforms as national securities exchanges, nor does Coinbase operate the Coinbase Digital Asset Platforms pursuant to an exemption from registration. The Complaint further alleges that Coinbase’s failure to so register is a violation of Section 5 of the Exchange Act.
On January 11, 2022, the Court issued an Order appointing Lead Plaintiffs and Counsel. Lead Plaintiffs filed an amended Complaint on March 11. Defendants filed a Motion to Dismiss the amended Complaint on May 10.
On February 1, 2023, the Court issued an Order granting Defendants' Motion to Dismiss. The case was dismissed with prejudice. Lead Plaintiffs filed a notice appealing the Court's Dismissal Order on February 9.
On April 5, 2024, the Court of Appeals issued an Order reversing in part and affirming in part the judgment of the District Court. The case was remanded for further proceedings.
On July 29, 2024, Defendants filed a Motion for Judgment on the Pleadings. On February 7, 2025, the Court issued an Order denying Defendants' Motion for Judgment.

According to the Complaint, Coinbase Global, Inc. is a cryptocurrency exchange.
On April 14, 2021, Coinbase went public through a direct offering, making available 114,850,769 shares of its Class A common stock to the general public.
The Complaint alleges that the registration statement and prospectus used to effectuate the Company’s Offering were false and misleading and omitted to state that, at the time of the Offering: (1) Coinbase required a sizeable cash injection; (2) Coinbase’s platform was susceptible to service-level disruptions, which were increasingly likely to occur as the Company scaled its services to a larger user base; and (3) as a result of the foregoing, the positive statements about the Company’s business, operations and prospects were materially misleading and/or lacked a reasonable basis.
On July 23, 2021, Plaintiff filed a corrected Complaint. The Court issued an Order consolidating cases and appointing Lead Plaintiff and Counsel on November 5.  All future docketing was ordered to be done in the lead case 21-CV-05634. On December 20, Lead Plaintiff filed a consolidated Complaint.
On June 20, 2023, the Court issued an Order staying the case pending the outcome of a case in the Ninth Circuit. The Court lifted the stay on February 25, 2025.
Lead Plaintiff voluntarily dismissed this case on March 5, 2025.

risk_card.data_coverage_note: SCA database contains ~6,982 filings (~35% of estimated universe). NERA/Cornerstone are authoritative full-universe references.

screening_questions.question: Is this a repeat SCA target? How many prior filings, and what were the outcomes?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: 25% of all SCAs involve companies sued before. 46 companies have been sued 4+ times. Prior history is a base-rate multiplier — plaintiffs bar has institutional memory.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: No prior SCAs. Or: 1 prior SCA that was dismissed, >5 years ago.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Any prior settled SCA. 2+ prior filings regardless of outcome. Active ongoing SCA.

screening_questions.question: Has the stock experienced a >15% single-day drop in the last 12 months?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Stock drops >15% are the mechanical trigger for plaintiffs firms to evaluate filing. Larger drops = larger damages = higher settlement.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: No single-day drop >10% in 12 months. Low volatility stock.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Any single-day drop >15%. Multiple drops >10%. Stock down >30% from 12-month high.

screening_questions.question: What is short interest as a % of float? Has it increased >50% in the last 90 days?
screening_questions.data_source: FINRA short interest data, short seller report databases
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Rising short interest is a leading indicator of both stock drops and short-seller reports that trigger SCAs (Hercules Capital/HTGC pattern).
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Short interest <3% of float, stable or declining.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Short interest >8% of float, or any increase >50% in 90 days. Named in short seller report.

screening_questions.question: What is the market cap? How does it map to the settlement severity curve for the relevant scenario?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Market cap is the strongest predictor of settlement size — its the damages denominator. A $50B company with a 20% drop has a $10B damages pool.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Market cap <$1B with simple business model — severity likely <$10M even if case settles.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Market cap >$10B — enters the zone where P90 settlements exceed $100M. Tower adequacy becomes critical.
